name: Laos
excerpt: Modern-day Laos has its roots in the ancient Lao kingdom of Lan Xang, established in the 14th century under King FA NGUM. For 300 years, Lan Xang had influence reaching into present-day Cambodia and Thailand, as well as over all of what is now Laos.

quick_facts.religions: Buddhist 64.7%, Christian 1.7%, none 31.4%, other/not stated 2.1% (2015 est.)
quick_facts.ethnic_groups: Lao 53.2%, Khmou 11%, Hmong 9.2%, Phouthay 3.4%, Tai 3.1%, Makong 2.5%, Katong 2.2%, Lue 2%, Akha 1.8%, other 11.6% (2015 est.)
quick_facts.climate: tropical monsoon; rainy season (May to November); dry season (December to April)
quick_facts.terrain: mostly rugged mountains; some plains and plateaus
quick_facts.natural_resources: timber, hydropower, gypsum, tin, gold, gemstones

summaries.geography_summary: landlocked; most of the country is mountainous and thickly forested; the Mekong River forms a large part of the western boundary with Thailand
summaries.history_summary: Modern-day Laos has its roots in the ancient Lao kingdom of Lan Xang, established in the 14th century under King FA NGUM. For 300 years, Lan Xang had influence reaching into present-day Cambodia and Thailand, as well as over all of what is now Laos. After centuries of gradual decline, Laos came under the domination of Siam (Thailand) from the late 18th century until the late 19th century, when it became part of French Indochina. The Franco-Siamese Treaty of 1907 defined the current Lao border with Thailand. Following more than 15 years of civil war, the communist Pathet Lao took control of the government in 1975, ending a six-century-old monarchy and instituting a one party--the Lao People's Revolutionary Party--communist state. A gradual, limited return to private enterprise and the liberalization of foreign investment laws began in the late 1980s. Laos became a member of ASEAN in 1997 and the WTO in 2013.In the 2010s, the country benefited from direct foreign investment, particularly in the natural resource and industry sectors. Construction of a number of large hydropower dams and expanding mining activities have also boosted the economy. Laos has retained its official commitment to communism and maintains close ties with its two communist neighbors, Vietnam and China, both of which continue to exert substantial political and economic influence on the country. China, for example, provided 70% of the funding for a $5.9 billion, 400-km railway line between the Chinese border and the capital Vientiane, which opened for operations in 2021. Laos financed the remaining 30% with loans from China. At the same time, Laos has expanded its economic reliance on the West and other Asian countries, such as Japan, Malaysia, Singapore, Taiwan, and Thailand. Nevertheless, despite steady economic growth for more than a decade, it remains one of Asia's poorest countries.
summaries.government_summary: communist party-led state. Independence: 19 July 1949 (from France); 22 October 1953 (Franco-Lao Treaty recognizes full independence)
summaries.economy_summary: lower middle-income, industrial Southeast Asian economy; high inflation due to 2022 currency depreciation brought on by persistently high debt; new Laos-China railway and dry port; rising inequities; ongoing labor shortages

suggested_reading: CIA World Factbook data (archived via factbook.json) | https://github.com/factbook/factbook.json

prayer_and_mission.prayer_intro: Laos is majority Buddhist under a communist government that restricts religious activity, with a small but present Christian minority. Pray for Laotian believers to remain faithful amid restriction.
prayer_points: Pray for Laos' small Christian minority to be strengthened and protected.
Pray for gospel witness to reach the Buddhist majority despite government restriction.
Pray for laborers willing to serve among Laos' many unreached ethnic groups.
prayer_and_mission.mission_emphasis: Laos' Buddhist majority and restrictive communist government make it a genuinely difficult field. Ongoing needs include discreet support for the existing Christian minority and continued prayer for open doors among Laos' many ethnic groups.
